transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hi Logan, I’m glad you’re here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Hey, thanks for having me. I guess... I’ve been thinking a lot about my relationship with Cleo lately. There’s been some stuff that’s been bothering me, but I’m not sure how to put it into words. It’s just... I keep seeing things that make me feel uneasy, and I don’t know if I’m overreacting or if there’s something more to it.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re feeling a bit unsettled about your relationship with Cleo, and it’s been on your mind a lot. It’s understandable to feel unsure about whether your feelings are justified. Can you share a bit more about what specifically has been bothering you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, I mean, it’s just... I keep noticing these messages on her phone from another guy. It’s like, every time I see it, I can’t help but think that maybe she’s not really into me, you know? Like, maybe she’s just using me or something. It makes me feel really sad and lonely. I don’t want to feel this way, but it’s hard not to when I see those messages.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds really painful, Logan. It’s tough to see something that raises doubts about your relationship, especially when it makes you feel sad and lonely. It seems like those messages have triggered some deep feelings of insecurity for you. Can you tell me more about what thoughts go through your mind when you see those messages?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, it’s like... my mind just goes to this dark place. I start thinking that she’ll just discard me like everyone else has in the past. It’s frustrating because I really care about her, but then I see those messages and it just feels like I’m not enough. I guess I just want to feel secure, but instead, I end up trying to make her jealous by going out with other women. It’s like I’m trying to prove something to myself, but it doesn’t really help. It just makes everything more complicated.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like those messages bring up a lot of old fears for you, especially the fear of being discarded, which can be really overwhelming. It’s understandable that you’d want to feel secure in your relationship with Cleo. I hear that you care about her, but you also feel the need to prove your worth, even if it complicates things further. How do you feel about the way you’re coping with those feelings by going out with other women?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Honestly, I’m not sure how I feel about it. Part of me thinks it’s a way to regain some control, like if I can make her jealous, maybe she’ll realize how much she needs me. But then, I also feel guilty about it. I don’t want to hurt her or make things worse between us. It’s just... I don’t know how else to deal with these feelings. It’s like I’m stuck in this cycle, and I can’t find a way out.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re caught in a difficult cycle, where your desire for control and validation conflicts with your care for Cleo and your feelings of guilt. It’s understandable to feel stuck when you’re trying to navigate those intense emotions. It seems like you’re aware that this approach might not be the healthiest, but you’re unsure of how to break free from it. What do you think might help you find a different way to cope with these feelings?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I’ve thought about that a bit, but it’s hard to know where to start. I guess I could try talking to Cleo about how I’m feeling, but that’s scary. What if she doesn’t feel the same way or thinks I’m being unreasonable? I just don’t want to push her away. Maybe I could find some other ways to distract myself or focus on something else, but I’m not sure what that would look like. It’s just... I want to feel better, but I don’t know how to get there.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
It’s completely understandable to feel scared about talking to Cleo, especially when you’re worried about how she might react. Opening up about your feelings can feel vulnerable, but it also seems like it could be a step toward clarity and connection. It’s also valid to think about distractions, but it sounds like you’re looking for something more meaningful to help you feel better. 

What do you think might be the biggest barrier for you in having that conversation with Cleo?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think the biggest barrier is probably my fear of rejection. I mean, what if she doesn’t understand where I’m coming from or thinks I’m being too clingy? It’s like I want to be honest, but I’m scared it’ll just push her away. Plus, there’s this part of me that worries that if I show my vulnerability, it’ll just confirm my fears that I’m not enough for her. It’s a tough spot to be in, feeling like I need to say something but also being terrified of the outcome.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
That fear of rejection sounds really heavy, and it makes sense that you’d feel torn between wanting to be honest and worrying about how Cleo might respond. It seems like your vulnerability feels risky, and you’re concerned it could reinforce those feelings of not being enough. It’s a tough position to be in, wanting to connect but feeling afraid of the potential consequences. 

What do you think would help you feel a bit more secure or supported as you consider having that conversation with her?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think having some support would really help. Maybe talking to a friend about it first could give me a little more confidence. If I could get some perspective from someone who knows me well, it might help me feel less alone in this. Also, just taking some time to really think about what I want to say could be useful. I guess I need to remind myself that it’s okay to be vulnerable and that it doesn’t mean I’m not enough. It’s just... hard to shake off those old fears.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
It sounds like reaching out to a friend for support could be a really helpful step for you. Having someone to talk to might provide you with the reassurance and perspective you need. Taking time to reflect on what you want to say is also a great idea, as it can help you feel more prepared and grounded. 

It’s completely normal to struggle with those old fears, especially when vulnerability is involved. It seems like you’re already recognizing that being vulnerable doesn’t diminish your worth. How do you think you might remind yourself of that when those fears start to creep in?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I could try to focus on the positive aspects of being open and honest. Maybe reminding myself that vulnerability can actually strengthen a relationship instead of weakening it. I could also write down some affirmations or things I appreciate about myself to counter those negative thoughts when they come up. Just having a little mantra or something to hold onto might help me push through the fear. It’s just about finding a way to shift my mindset, I guess.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
That sounds like a thoughtful approach, Logan. Focusing on the positive aspects of vulnerability and how it can strengthen your relationship is a powerful mindset shift. Writing down affirmations and things you appreciate about yourself could serve as a great reminder of your worth, especially when those negative thoughts arise. 

It seems like you’re really committed to finding a way to navigate this fear and create a healthier dynamic with Cleo. How do you feel about taking these steps, and what might be your first action moving forward?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel a bit more hopeful about it, actually. Just talking about it helps me see that there are ways to approach this instead of feeling stuck. I think my first action could be reaching out to that friend I mentioned. I’ll see if they’re available to chat soon. Just getting their perspective might help me feel more confident about talking to Cleo. After that, I’ll try to write down some affirmations and really think about what I want to say to her. It feels like a good starting point.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
I’m really glad to hear you’re feeling more hopeful, Logan. It sounds like reaching out to your friend is a solid first step, and it’s great that you’re thinking about how to prepare for your conversation with Cleo. Taking these actions can help you feel more empowered and less stuck. 

As you move forward, remember to be gentle with yourself through this process. It’s okay to take it one step at a time. Is there anything else you’d like to explore or discuss before we wrap up today?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thanks, I appreciate that. I think I’ve covered a lot for today. Just having this space to talk about everything has been really helpful. I guess I just want to keep reminding myself that it’s okay to take my time with this and that it’s a process. I’ll definitely keep those steps in mind as I move forward. Thank you for listening and guiding me through this.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You’re very welcome, Logan. I’m glad you found this space helpful, and it’s great to hear that you’re feeling more equipped to take your time with this process. Remember, it’s perfectly okay to take things at your own pace. If you ever want to talk more about this or anything else, I’m here for you. Take care of yourself as you move forward, and I wish you the best with your conversations ahead.

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you, I really appreciate that. It feels good to have a plan and some support. I’ll definitely keep that in mind as I navigate things with Cleo. I’ll take care of myself, and I’ll reach out if I need to talk again. Thanks for everything!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: I’m really glad to hear that, Logan. Having a plan and support can make a big difference. Remember, it’s all about taking those steps at your own pace. Take care of yourself, and don’t hesitate to reach out whenever you feel the need to talk again. Wishing you all the best with everything!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you so much! I really appreciate your support and understanding. It means a lot to me. I’ll keep working on things and take it one step at a time. Take care, and I’ll definitely reach out if I need to talk again.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You’re very welcome, Logan. I’m glad I could be here for you. It sounds like you have a positive mindset moving forward, and I’m confident you’ll navigate this journey well. Take care of yourself, and I look forward to hearing from you whenever you’re ready. Wishing you all the best!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you! That really means a lot. I’ll do my best to take care of myself and keep working through everything. I appreciate your encouragement and support. I’ll be in touch when I’m ready to talk again. Take care!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You’re welcome, Logan! I’m glad to hear that, and I’m here to support you whenever you need it. Take your time, and remember to be kind to yourself as you work through everything. I look forward to our next conversation. Take care!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thanks again! I really appreciate your kindness and support. I’ll keep that in mind as I move forward. Looking forward to our next conversation too. Take care!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You’re very welcome, Logan! I’m glad to have been here for you. Remember, you’re not alone in this, and it’s okay to take things one step at a time. I look forward to our next conversation as well. Take care of yourself!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you! It’s comforting to know I’m not alone in this. I’ll definitely take things one step at a time. I appreciate your support and look forward to talking again. Take care!
